findings.message: D2 lists direct inner attributes like `#![doc(hidden)]` as required detections, but then says the judgment target is any `syn item`. A crate/file-level inner attribute lives on `syn::File::attrs`, not on an item, so an implementation that follows the item-only wording can miss the exact inner-attribute form the ADR says is in scope. The plan should explicitly include file-level attrs as scan targets, or narrow the inner-attribute claim.

The same sentence says doc comment string literals do not appear as attributes in syn, but Rust doc comments are represented as `#[doc = "..."]` attributes. The intended behavior can still exclude them, but the ADR should describe that as ignoring `doc` name-value attributes and only matching `doc(hidden)` list forms, not as relying on doc comments being absent from the AST.

T001 is mapped to IN-04/AC-07, which require a reusable syn scanning helper with callback-swappable detection logic, but the implementation plan and infrastructure catalogue only define `doc_hidden::verify` and place file discovery, `syn::parse_file`, cfg-test filtering, and attribute detection inside that detector. That leaves no task/type contract for the common helper the spec requires, so the plan can satisfy the doc-hidden gate while missing the reusable scanning architecture.

The infrastructure catalogue declares no trait implementation changes, but T002 explicitly requires adding `verify_doc_hidden` to `FsVerifyAdapter`'s `VerifyPort` implementation. This makes the type catalogue/contract map inconsistent with the hexagonal wiring plan and can leave the new `VerifyPort` method without a documented infrastructure adapter implementation.

T001 narrows the scanner callback to crate-level attributes plus attributes on `Item` / impl-associated items, but the spec requires failing whenever any non-test source contains a `#[doc(hidden)]`-equivalent attribute. Attributes on other Rust AST nodes such as struct fields, enum variants, and trait associated items are still non-test source attributes and can affect rustdoc output, yet this traversal plan and its tests would not exercise them. As written, the gate can pass with prohibited `#[doc(hidden)]` declarations still present, so AC-02/AC-04 are not actually satisfied.

IN-01 explicitly includes adding the verify gate-name label, but T002 only plans the enum variant, dispatch arm, items_dir arm, and Makefile tasks. In this CLI, verify commands are also matched by a telemetry gate-name function, so following this plan as written leaves the new DocHidden variant without its required gate label and the wiring is incomplete. Add the gate-name label/update to T002 and the rendered plan/type artifacts so IN-01 is actually covered.

The type view now marks the CLI `VerifyCommand` row, including the `DocHidden` variant planned under T002, as 🔵, while the same plan still leaves T002 unchecked/todo in `plan.md` line 29 and `impl-plan.json` lines 10-12. These artifacts now assert both that the CLI wiring is complete and that the wiring task has not started, so the plan state is incoherent.

The `VerifyInput` row is now 🔵 for the `DocHidden` CLI-driver wiring that belongs to T002, but T002 remains todo in the rendered and JSON plan. This creates the same lifecycle contradiction for the cli-driver layer: the catalogue view says the T002 surface is complete while the task tracking says it is still unstarted.

The usecase type view now marks `VerifyPort`/`VerifyService` with `verify_doc_hidden` as 🔵 even though T002, which owns adding that usecase wiring, remains todo in `plan.md` and `impl-plan.json`. The type catalogue signals should not advance this wiring independently of the task state, otherwise the plan artifacts give conflicting implementation status.

T002 is now marked `in_progress`, but the transition described for this plan-artifacts round is only T001 moving from `todo` to `in_progress`, and T002's own description still declares T001 as its prerequisite. This leaves the plan claiming a dependent wiring task is active before the detection-core task is completed; keep T002 as `todo` until T001 is transitioned.

This rendered type view was changed from a pending signal to blue during a round whose stated artifact transition is limited to `impl-plan.json` and the generated `plan.md`; the same unexpected signal flip appears in `cli_driver-types.md`, `infrastructure-types.md`, and `usecase-types.md`. That makes the artifact set inconsistent with the declared lifecycle-only transition and should be restored/regenerated so type rendered views remain unchanged in this round.

`layer.path` from `architecture-rules.json` is joined directly into `root.join(...).join("src")` with no containment check, so a malformed or malicious layer entry can escape the trusted root (for example `../..` or an absolute path) and make the verifier scan arbitrary files outside the workspace.

The directory walk reads and recurses through entries with plain `read_dir`, `path.is_dir()`, and `read_to_string()` but never rejects symlinks at the boundary, so a symlink under `src/` can redirect the scan outside the workspace. This breaks the real-file semantics used elsewhere for trusted-root loaders.

File-backed modules are never resolved here. A crate with `#[cfg(test)] mod tests;` and a separate `tests.rs` under `src/` will still have that file scanned, so `#[doc(hidden)]` inside a test-only module can be reported as a violation. This breaks the stated `cfg(test) mod foo;` exclusion and needs module-file resolution or an equivalent skip map.
